title: The Bible: A Biography
author: Karen Armstrong
excerpt: A concise biography of the Bible as a living document, tracing its composition, canonization, and interpretation by Jews and Christians across 2,500 years.
keyIdeas: The Bible is not a single book but an anthology shaped over centuries by political and social forces
Interpretation of scripture has always been a creative act, not passive reception
The 'principle of charity' — reading scripture charitably — was the dominant hermeneutical approach for most of history
Literal interpretation of the Bible is a modern development, not an ancient tradition
Jewish midrash and Christian exegesis both treated scripture as a living, adaptable text
keyTakeaways: Understanding of how the 66 books of the Bible were written, collected, and canonized
Appreciation for 2,000 years of Jewish and Christian interpretive traditions
Insight into how political contexts shaped biblical interpretation from antiquity to modernity
A nuanced perspective on fundamentalism as a reaction to modernity rather than a return to tradition
whoShouldRead: Readers of religious history, students of biblical studies, and anyone curious about how the Bible became the world's most influential book
whoShouldSkip: Those seeking a verse-by-verse commentary or devotional guide; specialists requiring detailed textual criticism
relatedBooks: A History of God — Karen Armstrong
The Battle for God — Karen Armstrong
Who Wrote the Bible? — Richard Elliott Friedman
